questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of bilateral shoulder and hip pain and stiffness. The stiffness lasts over an hour each morning and is associated with difficulty getting out of a chair or combing her hair. She reports no joint swelling, rash, or weakness. Blood tests show ESR 65 mm/hr and CRP 45 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP are negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The patient has classic PMR: age >50, bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness >30 minutes, elevated acute phase reactants, and no synovitis or autoantibodies. This pattern distinguishes PMR from rheumatoid arthritis (which typically involves small joint synovitis) and osteoarthritis (stiffness shorter, pain with activity). Fibromyalgia lacks inflammatory markers, and polymyositis presents with weakness, not pain.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man presents with a 4-week history of throbbing headache, scalp tenderness, and jaw claudication when chewing. He also reports bilateral shoulder stiffness and difficulty raising his arms. On examination, the temporal arteries are tender and nodular. Temperature is 37.8°C. ESR is 90 mm/hr. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Polymyalgia rheumatica with giant cell arteritis
questions.explanation: The patient has both PMR symptoms (shoulder stiffness) and cranial GCA symptoms (headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication) with a tender temporal artery. PMR and GCA commonly co-exist. Temporal arteritis is a synonym for GCA, but the presence of PMR features makes 'PMR with GCA' the most accurate diagnosis. Polymyositis would present with weakness.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of progressive proximal muscle stiffness, fatigue, weight loss of 6 kg, and low-grade fevers. She has difficulty turning in bed and getting up from a chair. Examination reveals no synovitis or rash. ESR is 85 mm/hr, CRP 60 mg/L, and haemoglobin 10.5 g/dL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Remitting seronegative symmetrical synovitis with pitting oedema (RS3PE)
questions.explanation: PMR can present with prominent constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fever, fatigue) that mimic malignancy or infection. The key is the proximal girdle stiffness and elevated inflammatory markers without synovitis or oedema. RS3PE typically has pitting oedema of hands/feet, and rheumatoid arthritis would show synovitis.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with 2 months of morning stiffness in his hands, wrists, and shoulders lasting several hours. He also complains of hip pain. On examination, there is symmetrical synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints. No rash or subcutaneous nodules. ESR is 60 mm/hr, rheumatoid factor positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Symmetrical small joint synovitis with positive rheumatoid factor is characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis. PMR typically spares small joints and lacks synovitis. CPPD usually affects knees or wrists with acute attacks. Osteoarthritis is non-inflammatory and gout is episodic monoarthritis.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with difficulty rising from a chair and climbing stairs over 6 weeks. She denies any pain or stiffness. On examination, there is proximal muscle weakness in both shoulders and hips. Sensation is normal. Blood tests show ESR 28 mm/hr, CRP 10 mg/L, and CK 1800 U/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Proximal muscle weakness without pain, markedly elevated CK, and normal inflammatory markers suggest polymyositis rather than PMR. PMR typically presents with pain and stiffness, not isolated weakness, and CK is normal. Hypothyroidism and statins may cause myopathy but rarely such high CK without precipitating factors. Motor neurone disease shows fasciculations and mixed signs.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness for 6 weeks, worse in the morning. Her CRP is 45 mg/L and ESR 60 mm/hr. According to the 2012 ACR/EULAR classification criteria for polymyalgia rheumatica, which of the following is a mandatory entry criterion?
questions.explanation: The 2012 ACR/EULAR classification criteria require age ≥50 years as an essential entry criterion; the other items contribute points but are not mandatory for classification.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with proximal muscle pain and stiffness. On examination, there is mild weakness of hip flexors. Her CRP is 30 mg/L and ESR 50 mm/hr. Which single investigation is most useful to differentiate polymyalgia rheumatica from polymyositis?
questions.explanation: CK is typically normal in PMR but elevated in polymyositis. Anti-Jo-1 is specific for antisynthetase syndrome but less common; EMG and biopsy are second-line. CK is the initial discriminating test.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with suspected polymyalgia rheumatica has normal inflammatory markers. An ultrasound of both shoulders shows bilateral subacromial/subdeltoid bursitis and long head of biceps tenosynovitis. How does this finding affect the diagnosis?
options: It indicates a rotator cuff tear
It is highly suggestive of PMR
questions.explanation: Bilateral subacromial/subdeltoid bursitis and biceps tenosynovitis on ultrasound are characteristic of PMR, especially in the appropriate clinical context, and support the diagnosis even with normal inflammatory markers.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with known polymyalgia rheumatica on prednisolone 10 mg daily presents with new-onset severe headache, scalp tenderness, and transient vision loss in the right eye. Which investigation is the gold standard for confirming giant cell arteritis?
questions.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy remains the gold standard for diagnosing GCA, with giant cell arteritis on histology. ESR and CRP are non-specific; imaging may support but biopsy is definitive.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man has typical symptoms of polymyalgia rheumatica but his ESR is 15 mm/hr and CRP is 3 mg/L. Which investigation is most likely to provide objective evidence to support the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Ultrasound can detect subdeltoid bursitis and tenosynovitis in PMR even when acute phase reactants are normal, providing objective support for the diagnosis. MRI may show similar but is less accessible and often not first-line.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with bilateral shoulder and hip pain and stiffness lasting over an hour in the morning. Her ESR is 58 mm/hr and CRP 45 mg/L. Examination reveals no joint swelling or tenderness. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend oral prednisolone (15-20 mg daily) as first-line therapy for polymyalgia rheumatica. Methotrexate is used as a steroid-sparing agent in refractory cases, not first-line. NSAIDs provide only symptomatic relief and do not control the underlying inflammation. Hydroxychloroquine and leflunomide are not indicated for PMR.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known polymyalgia rheumatica on prednisolone 15 mg daily presents with a two-day history of severe headache, jaw claudication, and scalp tenderness. He also reports transient visual loss in his right eye. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Triamcinolone acetonide injection into the temporal artery
questions.explanation: The patient has features of giant cell arteritis, a medical emergency. Immediate high-dose prednisolone (40-60 mg daily or IV methylprednisolone if severe) is required to prevent permanent vision loss. Continuing the low dose or using steroid-sparing agents is inadequate. Biopsy can be done within a week without delaying treatment.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with polymyalgia rheumatica has been on prednisolone 15 mg daily for 6 months. She has developed significant steroid side effects including weight gain, hypertension, and osteopenia. Her PMR symptoms are well-controlled. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Add methotrexate as a steroid-sparing agent
Continue prednisolone and add alendronic acid only
questions.explanation: In patients with steroid side effects, NICE recommends considering a steroid-sparing agent such as methotrexate to reduce the cumulative steroid dose. Rapid tapering may cause relapse. Leflunomide and infliximab are not indicated for PMR. Alendronic acid is for bone protection but does not reduce steroid dose.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman presents with bilateral shoulder and hip pain, morning stiffness, raised ESR and CRP. She has a history of severe osteoporosis (T-score -3.5) and a previous vertebral fracture. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her polymyalgia rheumatica?
options: Prednisolone 15 mg daily plus alendronic acid 70 mg weekly
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids are first-line for PMR, but in patients with high fracture risk, bisphosphonate prophylaxis (e.g., alendronic acid) should be co-prescribed to prevent steroid-induced osteoporosis. Methotrexate is not first-line. NSAIDs and hydroxychloroquine are not appropriate disease-modifying therapy.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with well-controlled type 2 diabetes presents with typical polymyalgia rheumatica symptoms, ESR 50 mm/hr, CRP 38 mg/L. He is on metformin. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Prednisolone 15 mg daily with close glucose monitoring
questions.explanation: Prednisolone remains first-line therapy even in patients with diabetes, but glucose levels must be monitored closely and diabetic medication may need adjustment. Methotrexate is not first-line. Lower doses or NSAIDs are insufficient to control inflammation. Hydroxychloroquine is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain, early morning stiffness lasting >1 hour, and difficulty rising from a chair. She reports fatigue and low-grade fever. Examination reveals symmetrical shoulder and hip stiffness with no synovitis or rash. Bloods show ESR 78 mm/h, CRP 65 mg/L, normal CK and RF. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In a patient with typical PMR symptoms and raised inflammatory markers, immediate treatment with prednisolone is recommended to relieve symptoms and prevent progression. Temporal artery imaging is reserved for suspected GCA; anti-CCP is for rheumatoid arthritis; naproxen is insufficient; rheumatology referral can follow.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with PMR diagnosed 6 months ago is currently on prednisolone 10 mg daily. His symptoms are well-controlled but any attempt to taper below 10 mg results in recurrence of shoulder stiffness and raised ESR. He has no new headache or visual symptoms. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: When PMR cannot be tapered below a threshold dose (usually 7.5–10 mg/day), a steroid-sparing agent such as methotrexate should be considered per NICE guidelines. Increasing prednisolone would perpetuate steroid dependence; biopsy is for GCA; ultrasound is not diagnostic; alendronic acid is for bone protection but does not address the weaning failure.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with known PMR on prednisolone 15 mg daily presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of severe right-sided headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication when chewing, and transient loss of vision in the right eye lasting 5 minutes. Her temperature is 37.8°C. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: The symptoms suggest giant cell arteritis (GCA) complicating PMR. High-dose IV steroids must be given immediately to prevent irreversible vision loss. CT head is not indicated as the presentation is typical for GCA; temporal artery imaging can be done after steroids; checking inflammatory markers delays treatment; ophthalmology referral is secondary.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with PMR treated with prednisolone 15 mg daily for 4 months reports new-onset difficulty rising from a chair and climbing stairs. She has no shoulder or hip pain and no headache. Examination shows proximal muscle weakness (4/5) in both legs. ESR is 12 mm/h and CRP is 3 mg/L. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In a patient on steroids with proximal weakness but normal inflammatory markers, steroid-induced myopathy is likely. Checking CK helps differentiate from PMR relapse (normal CK in myopathy, elevated in inflammatory myositis). Increasing steroids would worsen myopathy; EMG may be needed later; DEXA and bisphosphonates are for osteoporosis prevention, not acute weakness.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with well-controlled PMR on prednisolone 7.5 mg daily presents with acute-onset severe low back pain after lifting a heavy box. He has no neurological symptoms. He has been on prednisolone for 8 months. DEXA scan 3 months ago showed T-score of -2.8 at the hip. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Check serum calcium and vitamin D
questions.explanation: Given long-term steroid use and known osteoporosis, this presentation raises concern for vertebral fragility fracture. Plain X-ray is the first-line investigation for suspected osteoporotic fracture. Alendronic acid is indicated but not the immediate next step for acute back pain; reducing steroids may cause PMR flare; MRI is second-line; calcium/vitamin D are baseline but X-ray takes priority.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of bilateral shoulder and hip stiffness, worse in the morning, and raised ESR of 85 mm/hr. She also reports a new-onset left-sided headache, jaw claudication when eating, and a transient episode of blurred vision in her right eye lasting 5 minutes. Which of the following complications is most important to recognise urgently?
options: Giant cell arteritis with anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy
questions.explanation: The combination of jaw claudication, headache, and transient visual loss in an elderly patient with polymyalgia rheumatica is highly suggestive of giant cell arteritis. Urgent recognition is vital to prevent permanent vision loss. The most tempting distractor is migraine with aura, but the age and associated systemic features make GCA far more likely.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with established polymyalgia rheumatica, well-controlled on prednisolone 10 mg daily for 6 months, presents with acute-onset severe right hip pain. He is unable to weight bear and has marked restriction of internal rotation. He has no fever. Which of the following is the most likely complication?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
questions.explanation: Acute severe hip pain with limited internal rotation in a patient on chronic steroids is a classic presentation of avascular necrosis. The most tempting distractor is septic arthritis, but the absence of fever and the specific movement restriction point to AVN.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with polymyalgia rheumatica on prednisolone 7.5 mg daily for 4 months presents to the emergency department with a 1-day history of epigastric pain and vomiting. She reports passing black, tarry stools this morning. Her pulse is 110 bpm and BP 90/60 mmHg. Which of the following is the most important diagnosis to exclude?
questions.explanation: The combination of epigastric pain, vomiting, melaena, and haemodynamic instability in a patient on corticosteroids is highly suggestive of a perforated peptic ulcer. Steroids increase the risk of peptic ulcer disease and perforation. The most tempting distractor is acute pancreatitis, but melaena points to an upper GI source.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with bilateral shoulder pain and stiffness, raised ESR of 90 mm/hr, and low-grade fever. He has also lost 6 kg in weight over 2 months and reports drenching night sweats. Examination reveals axillary lymphadenopathy. Initial treatment with prednisolone 15 mg daily has provided minimal improvement after 1 week. Which of the following diagnoses must be considered before confirming polymyalgia rheumatica?
questions.explanation: Systemic symptoms such as weight loss, night sweats, lymphadenopathy, and poor response to steroids are red flags for an underlying malignancy, which can mimic polymyalgia rheumatica. The most tempting distractor is rheumatoid arthritis, but the symmetrical proximal stiffness is more typical of PMR, and the systemic symptoms point to malignancy.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with recently diagnosed polymyalgia rheumatica on prednisolone 20 mg daily for 2 weeks presents with a hot, swollen, painful right knee. She is unable to bear weight and has a temperature of 38.5°C. Her right knee is erythematous, with effusion and extreme pain on passive movement. Which of the following is the most important diagnosis to exclude?
questions.explanation: A hot, swollen, painful joint with fever and inability to bear weight in an immunocompromised patient (on steroids) is septic arthritis until proven otherwise. Even though gout and pseudogout are possible, septic arthritis is an emergency requiring urgent aspiration and antibiotics. The most tempting distractor is gout, but the fever and rapid onset in an immunosuppressed patient mandate excluding infection first.
